rules.title: A rollback path is documented
rules.lesson: the rollback plan must exist before the deploy that needs it — "better to have a well-thought out plan in place instead of trying to make one up when your service is on fire". Holds at solo. Deploy-scoped twin of baseline OPS-05
rules.rationale: The rollback plan must exist before the deploy that needs it — the strongest sentence in the corpus, and it holds at solo (the tier whose whole posture is fast recovery). Deploy-scoped twin of baseline OPS-05: this scans for revert/redeploy steps, not a generic runbook.
rules.fix: Add a rollback section to the README or a runbook: how to revert/redeploy-previous for this repo’s deploy mechanism (templates/runbook-rollback.md).

rules.title: Rollback is verified by drill
rules.lesson: a documented-but-never-exercised rollback is the named anti-pattern; recovery capability is demonstrated, not asserted. Three primary sources converge — sign-off-only is deliberate (a drill can’t be observed at rest)
rules.rationale: A documented-but-never-exercised rollback is the named anti-pattern; recovery capability is demonstrated, not asserted. Three independent primary sources converge, so the sign-off-only design is deliberate: the checker can’t observe a drill at rest, so a dated judgment with a tripwire is the honest mechanism.
rules.fix: Run a rollback drill (roll back the last deploy in staging, time it, note traps), then pipeline jdg new --kind sign-off --subject RB-02 --reason "drilled <date>: <what happened>" --review-by <date>.

rules.title: A mechanical fast-rollback path exists
rules.lesson: "easy to perform" is the mechanical property checked at rest — a rollback that requires re-engineering the deploy under pressure is not a rollback path. "trusted to be low-risk" is RB-02’s drill residue
rules.rationale: "Easy to perform" is the mechanical property this check looks for at rest — a rollback that requires re-engineering the deploy under pressure is not a rollback path. A workflow_dispatch version input enabling redeploy-previous, or a documented revert-and-redeploy flow. Heuristic.
rules.fix: Add a workflow_dispatch version input to the deploy workflow that deploys an arbitrary prior ref; or document the revert-and-redeploy flow precisely in the runbook.

rules.title: A recovery objective is declared and reviewed
rules.lesson: recovery time is a first-class measured objective; declare-measure-review is precisely this ledger entry. Narrowed: sources support measure-and-review, not blanket gates. The declaration is machine-readable; whether it is met is human judgment. DORA renamed MTTR → FDRT (2023)
rules.rationale: Recovery time is a first-class measured objective, and the SRE governance pattern — declare an objective, measure it, review the gap against actuals — is precisely this ledger entry. The declaration (descriptor recovery_objective) is machine-readable; whether it is met is dated human judgment. Absent objective yields WARN-level detail on the sign-off finding.
rules.fix: Declare recovery_objective in the descriptor (e.g. "failed deployment recovery time < 30 min"), then review it against the incident/drill record each period and sign off with evidence. Use the current DORA name (failed deployment recovery time).
